module.title: Module 4: Aviation Weather & Micrometeorology
module.description: How the atmosphere behaves and why it matters to a small drone: the standard atmosphere, temperature/dewpoint and humidity, density altitude and its drag on sUAS performance, atmospheric stability and fronts, clouds/ceilings/visibility and the 3 SM and cloud-clearance rules, wind and local terrain effects, thunderstorms, microbursts and wind shear, fog, and how to pull and decode a METAR and a TAF from the proper FAA weather sources.
learning_objectives: Describe the composition and layers of the atmosphere and the values of the ICAO standard atmosphere (15 degrees C, 29.92 inHg, ~2 degrees C per 1,000 ft lapse).
Relate temperature, dewpoint, and the temperature-dewpoint spread to relative humidity, cloud and fog formation.
Define density altitude, list the 'high, hot, humid' factors that raise it, and explain why high density altitude degrades sUAS lift, thrust, and endurance.
Distinguish stable from unstable air and identify the four front types and the weather each brings.
Connect clouds, ceilings, and visibility to the Part 107 minimums of 3 statute miles visibility and 500 ft below / 2,000 ft horizontal cloud clearance.
Recognize hazardous weather for a small UA: thunderstorms (three stages), microbursts and wind shear, gusts, and the fog types.
Decode every element of a METAR and a TAF and name the correct FAA weather sources (1800wxbrief / Leidos, aviationweather.gov).

lessons.title: The Atmosphere, the Standard Atmosphere & Moisture
learning_objectives: Describe the gas composition of the atmosphere and the role of the troposphere in weather.
State the values of the ICAO standard atmosphere and the standard temperature and pressure lapse rates.
Explain temperature, dewpoint, relative humidity, and the meaning of the temperature-dewpoint spread.

content.html:
Why weather is a Part 107 subject

Weather is roughly 11 to 16 percent of the Unmanned Aircraft General (UAG) knowledge exam, and it is the single biggest physical threat to a small drone. A multirotor weighs a few pounds and has very little inertia, so it is far more sensitive to wind, gusts, density altitude, and precipitation than a manned aircraft. You do not need to be a meteorologist, but you must understand why the air behaves as it does, recognize hazardous conditions before launch, and be able to read a METAR and a TAF. This module builds that understanding from the ground up.

What the atmosphere is made of

The atmosphere is a thin shell of gas held to Earth by gravity. By volume the dry atmosphere is about 78 percent nitrogen and 21 percent oxygen, with the remaining ~1 percent argon, carbon dioxide, and trace gases. Critically, the atmosphere also carries a variable amount of water vapor (up to a few percent), and it is that water vapor that drives clouds, precipitation, fog, and most of what we call "weather." Air has mass; the weight of all the air above a point creates atmospheric pressure, which decreases with altitude.

Layers of the atmosphere

The atmosphere is layered by how temperature changes with height. The lowest layer, the troposphere, extends from the surface to roughly 36,000 ft and is where essentially all weather and all sUAS flight occur. In the troposphere temperature normally decreases with altitude. Above it the stratosphere (containing the ozone layer) is more stable. A small drone never leaves the bottom slice of the troposphere, but the same physics that governs the whole layer governs the first 400 feet you fly in.

The ICAO standard atmosphere

To compare aircraft performance and calibrate instruments, aviation uses a fixed reference called the ICAO standard atmosphere. At sea level it defines:

  • Temperature = 15 degrees C (59 degrees F)
  • Pressure = 29.92 inches of mercury (1013.2 hPa)
  • Standard temperature lapse rate = about 2 degrees C per 1,000 ft of altitude gain
  • Standard pressure lapse rate = about 1 inHg per 1,000 ft in the lower atmosphere

Real conditions rarely match the standard exactly, and the difference between actual and standard conditions is exactly what produces density altitude (next lesson) and is why performance varies day to day.

content.alt: Diagram of the ICAO standard atmosphere showing sea-level values and the temperature lapse rate
content.caption: Figure: The ICAO standard atmosphere - sea-level temperature 15 degrees C, pressure 29.92 inHg, and a lapse rate of about 2 degrees C per 1,000 ft.

content.html:
Temperature, dewpoint, and relative humidity

Temperature measures the heat energy of the air. The dewpoint is the temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure) for it to become saturated - the point at which water vapor begins to condense into visible moisture. Relative humidity is the ratio of the water vapor present to the maximum the air could hold at that temperature, expressed as a percentage; at 100 percent relative humidity the temperature and dewpoint are equal and condensation occurs. Warm air can hold more water vapor than cold air, which is why cooling air toward its dewpoint is the universal trigger for clouds, fog, and dew.

content.text: The temperature-dewpoint SPREAD is your fog and low-cloud alarm. A small spread (temperature and dewpoint close together, e.g., 12/11) means the air is nearly saturated - expect fog, mist, or low ceilings. A large spread (e.g., 27/04) means dry air and clear skies. On a METAR, watch that pair of numbers closely.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The atmosphere is ~78% nitrogen and ~21% oxygen, plus variable water vapor that drives weather; pressure decreases with altitude.
Essentially all weather and all sUAS flight happen in the troposphere (surface to ~36,000 ft), where temperature normally falls with height.
ICAO standard atmosphere: 15 degrees C and 29.92 inHg at sea level, lapse ~2 degrees C per 1,000 ft - the reference for performance and density altitude.
Dewpoint is the temperature at which air saturates; relative humidity is how close the air is to saturation.
A SMALL temperature-dewpoint spread signals near-saturated air and likely fog or low cloud.

flashcards.definition: The lowest atmospheric layer (surface to ~36,000 ft) where virtually all weather and all sUAS operations occur and where temperature normally decreases with altitude.
A fixed reference atmosphere: 15 degrees C and 29.92 inHg at sea level, with a temperature lapse of about 2 degrees C per 1,000 ft. Used to compare performance and calibrate altimeters.
The temperature to which air must be cooled, at constant pressure, to become saturated (relative humidity 100%) so that water vapor begins to condense into cloud, fog, or dew.
The amount of water vapor in the air expressed as a percentage of the maximum the air could hold at that temperature; 100% means temperature equals dewpoint.
The difference between the temperature and the dewpoint. A small spread indicates near-saturated air and likely fog or low ceilings; a large spread indicates dry air.

images_needed.caption: Diagram of the ICAO standard atmosphere: a vertical scale showing sea-level temperature 15 degrees C and pressure 29.92 inHg, with the ~2 degrees C per 1,000 ft temperature lapse illustrated up through the troposphere.

lessons.title: Density Altitude & Its Effect on sUAS Performance
learning_objectives: Define pressure altitude and density altitude and explain how density altitude is computed conceptually.
List the three factors that raise density altitude ('high, hot, humid').
Explain in physical terms why high density altitude reduces lift, thrust, and endurance for a small drone.

content.html:
From pressure altitude to density altitude

Pressure altitude is the altitude you read when the altimeter is set to the standard datum of 29.92 inHg. Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature (and, to a lesser degree, humidity). In plain language, density altitude is the altitude the aircraft thinks it is flying at based on how thin the air actually is. When the air is less dense than standard, the drone performs as though it were at a higher elevation - even if it never leaves the ground a few feet.

What thins the air: high, hot, and humid

Air density falls when any of three things happen, summarized by the memory aid "high, hot, and humid":

  • High elevation / low pressure - fewer air molecules above you, so the air is thinner. A hot day in Denver is a classic high-density-altitude environment.
  • High temperature - warm air expands, so the molecules are spread farther apart and density drops. This is usually the biggest day-to-day driver.
  • High humidity - water vapor (molecular weight ~18) is lighter than the nitrogen and oxygen it displaces, so moist air is slightly less dense than dry air. The effect is smaller than temperature or elevation but it adds up.

All three raise density altitude, and a high density altitude degrades performance.

content.text: High, hot, and humid = high density altitude = LESS LIFT. On a hot, high, humid afternoon a propeller bites into thinner air, motors strain, and the same drone climbs more slowly, carries less payload, and drains its battery faster. Reduce your payload and plan for reduced endurance and sluggish climb.

content.html:
Why thin air hurts a small drone

A multirotor flies by accelerating air downward with its propellers; thrust and lift both depend directly on air density. When the air is thinner, each spin of the prop moves less mass of air, so:

  • Lift decreases - the rotors must spin faster (and draw more current) just to hover.
  • Thrust and climb suffer - the reserve power available for climbing or fighting wind shrinks.
  • Endurance drops - working the motors harder drains the battery faster, shortening flight time.
  • Payload capacity falls - the margin to lift a camera or sensor is reduced.

The net effect: on a high-density-altitude day, expect a sluggish, power-hungry, short-lived flight, and reduce weight accordingly.

content.alt: Chart relating temperature and pressure altitude to density altitude
content.caption: Figure: A density-altitude chart - enter with outside air temperature and pressure altitude to read density altitude, illustrating how heat and elevation raise it.

content.html:
Standard conditions as the baseline

Density altitude equals true altitude only under standard conditions (15 degrees C, 29.92 inHg at sea level, ~2 degrees C per 1,000 ft). When the actual temperature is warmer than standard for your elevation, density altitude is higher than your true altitude; when it is colder than standard, density altitude is lower and performance actually improves. This is why a drone often feels strong and climbs eagerly on a cold winter morning and feels weak on a hot summer afternoon at the same field.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature (and humidity); it is the altitude the aircraft 'feels.'
'High, hot, and humid' all RAISE density altitude and HURT performance.
Thin air means each propeller stroke moves less mass, so lift, thrust, climb, payload, and endurance all decrease.
Warmer-than-standard air gives a HIGHER density altitude than your true altitude; colder-than-standard gives a lower one and better performance.
On a high-density-altitude day, reduce payload and plan for slower climb and shorter flight time.

flashcards.definition: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature (and humidity); the altitude at which the aircraft performs given the actual air density. High density altitude means thin air and degraded performance.
The altitude indicated when the altimeter setting is the standard datum 29.92 inHg; the starting point from which density altitude is computed.

The memory aid for the three factors that raise density altitude - high elevation/low pressure, high temperature, and high humidity - all of which thin the air and reduce sUAS lift, thrust, and endurance.

flashcards.term: Effect of density altitude on sUAS
flashcards.definition: As density altitude rises, propellers move less air mass, so the drone produces less lift and thrust, climbs slower, carries less payload, and has shorter flight time.

images_needed.caption: A density-altitude conversion chart: outside air temperature on the horizontal axis, pressure-altitude lines diagonally, density altitude read off the vertical axis - annotated to show that a hot day at a high field yields a very high density altitude.

lessons.title: Stability, Air Masses & Fronts
learning_objectives: Distinguish stable from unstable air by lapse rate, cloud type, precipitation, visibility, and turbulence.
Define an air mass and the four types of fronts.
Predict the weather and wind shift associated with cold, warm, stationary, and occluded fronts.

content.html:
Atmospheric stability and lapse rates

Stability describes whether a parcel of air that is nudged upward keeps rising or sinks back. It is governed by the lapse rate - how fast the surrounding temperature falls with height. When the actual (environmental) lapse rate is steep (temperature drops quickly with altitude), a lifted parcel stays warmer than its surroundings and keeps rising: the air is unstable. When the lapse rate is shallow - or reverses in a temperature inversion (warmer air on top of cooler air) - a lifted parcel cools below its surroundings and sinks back: the air is stable.

content.text: A temperature inversion (warm air aloft over cool air below) is very stable and TRAPS haze, smoke, and pollutants near the surface - reducing visibility. Inversions also favor fog formation. Smooth air with poor visibility is a stable-air signature.

content.html:
Air masses

An air mass is a large body of air that is relatively uniform in temperature and moisture because it sat over a source region (e.g., cold dry Canadian land, warm moist Gulf water) long enough to take on its character. As an air mass moves, it modifies and, where it meets a different air mass, a front forms along the boundary. Fronts are where wind shifts, clouds build, precipitation falls, and turbulence concentrates - so they are the main features a pilot tracks.

The four front types

A front is the boundary between two air masses. There are four kinds:

  • Cold front - cold air advances and undercuts warmer air, shoving it up steeply. Produces a narrow band of often-violent weather: towering cumulus and thunderstorms, heavy showers, gusty shifting winds, then rapid clearing with cooler, drier, more unstable air behind. Cold fronts move fast.
  • Warm front - warm air advances and rides up over retreating cold air on a shallow slope. Produces a wide region of layered stratiform clouds, low ceilings, steady precipitation, and poor visibility well ahead of the front; moves slowly.
  • Stationary front - neither air mass advances; the boundary stalls, often bringing prolonged clouds and precipitation over the same area for days.
  • Occluded front - a fast cold front overtakes a warm front, lifting the warm air completely off the surface. Combines characteristics of both, with widespread clouds and precipitation.

content.alt: Diagram of cold, warm, stationary, and occluded fronts with their map symbols and cloud cross-sections
content.caption: Figure: The four fronts - cold (blue triangles), warm (red half-circles), stationary (alternating), and occluded (purple) - with cross-sections showing cloud build-up and the steep vs. shallow frontal slopes.

content.text: For a small drone, the most dangerous moment is the passage of a fast-moving COLD front: a sudden wind shift, strong gusts, and embedded thunderstorms can arrive abruptly. If a cold front is forecast, do not be in the air when it passes. A wind shift and pressure/temperature change are the hallmark of any frontal passage.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Stability depends on the lapse rate: steep lapse = unstable (cumuliform clouds, showers, good visibility, turbulence); shallow lapse/inversion = stable (stratiform clouds, steady precip, poor visibility, smooth air).
A temperature inversion is very stable and traps haze/fog, reducing visibility.
An air mass is a large uniform body of air; a front is the boundary where two air masses meet.
Cold fronts bring narrow, violent, fast weather and clearing behind; warm fronts bring wide, layered, slow weather with low ceilings ahead.
Stationary fronts stall (prolonged weather); occluded fronts form when a cold front overtakes a warm front. Every frontal passage brings a wind shift.

flashcards.definition: Air resistant to vertical motion (shallow lapse rate or inversion). Produces stratiform clouds, steady precipitation, poor visibility (trapped haze/fog), and smooth air.
Air that, once lifted, keeps rising (steep lapse rate). Produces cumuliform clouds, showery precipitation, good visibility, and turbulence/gusty winds.
A layer where temperature increases with altitude (warm over cold). Very stable; traps haze, smoke, and moisture near the surface, reducing visibility and favoring fog.
A large body of air with relatively uniform temperature and moisture acquired from its source region; where two air masses meet, a front forms.
A boundary where cold air advances under warmer air, lifting it steeply; brings narrow, often violent weather (thunderstorms, gusts, wind shift) and clearing, cooler, drier air behind.
A boundary where warm air rides up over retreating cold air on a shallow slope; brings widespread stratiform clouds, low ceilings, steady precipitation, and poor visibility ahead of it.

images_needed.caption: A four-panel fronts diagram showing cold, warm, stationary, and occluded fronts: their standard weather-map symbols (blue triangles, red half-circles, alternating symbols, purple combined) plus a side-view cross-section of each showing the air-mass slopes and the resulting cloud and precipitation pattern.

lessons.title: Clouds, Ceilings, Visibility & the Cloud-Clearance Rules
learning_objectives: Identify the basic cloud families and their height classes.
Define sky-cover terms FEW, SCT, BKN, OVC and the meaning of a ceiling.
Connect clouds, ceilings, and visibility to the Part 107 minimums of 3 SM visibility and 500 ft below / 2,000 ft horizontal cloud clearance.

content.html:
Cloud families

Clouds are condensed water droplets or ice crystals that form when air is cooled to its dewpoint. They come in two basic shapes - layered stratus (stable air) and puffy cumulus (unstable air) - and are classified by height into low, middle (prefix "alto"), and high (prefix "cirro") families. The prefix or suffix nimbo/nimbus means the cloud is producing precipitation. The towering cumulonimbus is the thunderstorm cloud and is the single cloud type a drone pilot must avoid entirely.

Sky cover and the ceiling

METARs report how much of the sky is covered in oktas (eighths), using four codes plus a height in hundreds of feet above ground level (AGL):

  • FEW = 1 to 2 oktas (few clouds)
  • SCT = 3 to 4 oktas (scattered)
  • BKN = 5 to 7 oktas (broken)
  • OVC = 8 oktas (overcast)
  • SKC / CLR = sky clear

A ceiling is defined as the height of the lowest layer reported as broken (BKN) or overcast (OVC) - or the vertical visibility into an obscuration. FEW and SCT layers are not ceilings. So in a report of "SCT020 BKN040 OVC080," the ceiling is 4,000 ft AGL (the lowest BKN/OVC layer).

content.text: Cloud heights in a METAR are AGL and in HUNDREDS of feet. BKN012 means a broken ceiling at 1,200 ft AGL. FEW250 means a few clouds at 25,000 ft (cirrus). The lowest BKN or OVC layer is the ceiling - FEW and SCT never count.

content.html:
The regulatory link: 3 SM and 500/2,000

Cloud and visibility data are not academic - they decide whether you may legally fly. Under 14 CFR 107.51, a Remote PIC must maintain:

  • Minimum flight visibility of 3 statute miles, measured from the control-station location.
  • Cloud clearance of at least 500 ft below the clouds and at least 2,000 ft horizontally from the clouds.

So a low ceiling or reduced visibility can ground you even on a calm day. If a METAR shows visibility below 3 SM, or a ceiling so low that 400-ft AGL flight cannot stay 500 ft below the clouds, the operation is not legal. These minimums exist so the Remote PIC and any nearby manned traffic can see and avoid each other.

content.text: Memorize: 3 SM visibility, 500 ft BELOW clouds, 2,000 ft HORIZONTAL from clouds. A common exam trap reverses these (e.g., '1,000 below / 500 horizontal' or '1 SM'). The numbers are 3 / 500 / 2,000 for Part 107.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Clouds form when air cools to its dewpoint; stratus = layered/stable, cumulus = puffy/unstable, nimbo/nimbus = precipitating, cumulonimbus = the thunderstorm cloud to avoid.
Sky cover: FEW (1-2 oktas), SCT (3-4), BKN (5-7), OVC (8); heights are AGL in hundreds of feet.
A ceiling is the lowest BKN or OVC layer (or vertical visibility into an obscuration); FEW and SCT are never ceilings.
Part 107 requires minimum 3 statute miles flight visibility from the control station.
Part 107 cloud clearance is at least 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds; low ceilings or low visibility can ground the flight.

flashcards.definition: The height above ground of the lowest cloud layer reported as broken (BKN) or overcast (OVC), or the vertical visibility into an obscuration. FEW and SCT layers do not count as a ceiling.
METAR sky-cover codes in oktas (eighths): FEW = 1-2, SCT (scattered) = 3-4, BKN (broken) = 5-7, OVC (overcast) = 8. Heights follow in hundreds of feet AGL.
A Remote PIC must maintain at least 3 statute miles of flight visibility, measured from the control-station location (14 CFR 107.51).
At least 500 feet below the clouds and at least 2,000 feet horizontally from the clouds (14 CFR 107.51).
The towering thunderstorm cloud, producing severe turbulence, lightning, hail, and microbursts. A drone pilot must avoid it entirely.

images_needed.caption: A cloud-family height chart showing low (stratus, stratocumulus, cumulus), middle (altostratus, altocumulus), and high (cirrus, cirrostratus) clouds plus a towering cumulonimbus, with the FEW/SCT/BKN/OVC sky-cover icons labeled.

lessons.title: Wind, Thunderstorms, Microbursts & Fog
learning_objectives: Explain how wind, gusts, and terrain/building effects threaten a small drone.
Describe the three stages of a thunderstorm and the hazards each presents.
Define a microburst and wind shear and explain why they are a serious sUAS hazard, and identify the common fog types.

content.html:
Wind and gusts

Wind is air moving from high pressure toward low pressure, deflected by the Earth's rotation. For a small drone, wind is the everyday hazard: a multirotor must constantly tilt and burn battery to hold position against it, and a gust (a sudden, brief increase in wind speed) can overpower a lightweight aircraft, push it off course, or exceed the maximum wind speed the airframe can handle (often only 20 to 25 mph for consumer drones - check the manufacturer's spec). Always compare the forecast and reported wind and gusts to your aircraft's limit before launch, and remember that wind speed increases with altitude.

Local effects near terrain and buildings

Near the surface, wind is shaped by obstacles. Air flowing over and around buildings, trees, hills, and ridgelines creates mechanical turbulence - swirling eddies, rotors, and rapid local wind shifts on the downwind (lee) side. Flying close to a building corner, over a rooftop edge, or in the lee of a ridge can produce sudden, invisible turbulence that destabilizes a drone. Channeling between buildings can also accelerate the wind locally. Plan launch, landing, and inspection paths to keep clear of these turbulent zones, especially on windy days.

content.text: Mechanical turbulence on the lee (downwind) side of buildings, ridges, and trees is strongest when winds are strong and the air is unstable. The turbulent eddies are invisible - anticipate them from the wind direction and the obstacle layout rather than waiting to feel them.

content.html:
Thunderstorms - three stages

A thunderstorm requires three ingredients: moisture, an unstable lapse rate, and a lifting force (a front, terrain, or heating). It develops through three stages:

  • Cumulus (building) stage - dominated by updrafts as the cloud towers upward; no precipitation reaching the ground yet.
  • Mature stage - the most violent stage; updrafts and downdrafts coexist, heavy rain/hail falls, lightning, and the strongest turbulence and wind shear occur. Mature is the stage to fear.
  • Dissipating stage - dominated by downdrafts; the storm rains itself out and weakens.

A drone has no business anywhere near a thunderstorm. Hazards - violent turbulence, lightning, hail, heavy rain, and microbursts - extend well beyond the visible cloud.

Microbursts and wind shear - a serious sUAS hazard

Wind shear is a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance, horizontally or vertically. A microburst is an intense, localized downdraft from a thunderstorm (or even a rain shower) that slams down and spreads out in all directions at the surface, producing extreme, rapidly reversing wind shear over a small area (typically less than 2.5 miles across) and lasting only minutes. For a manned aircraft a microburst is deadly; for a lightweight drone, the sudden downdraft and shifting horizontal winds can instantly flip it, drive it into the ground, or carry it away. Microbursts can occur with little visual warning - sometimes under a harmless-looking shower or virga. Treat any nearby convective activity as a microburst threat and stay grounded.

content.text: Microburst + wind shear = one of the most dangerous conditions for a small UA. The downdraft and explosive outflow can exceed any drone's control authority in seconds. Do not fly near thunderstorms, rain showers, or virga - the hazard can strike under clouds that look benign.

content.html:
Fog types

Fog is simply a cloud at the surface; it forms when the temperature-dewpoint spread closes to near zero. Know the common types:

  • Radiation fog - forms on clear, calm nights as the ground radiates heat and cools the air to its dewpoint; common in valleys at dawn, burns off after sunrise.
  • Advection fog - forms when warm, moist air moves horizontally over a cooler surface (e.g., coastal); needs some wind and can persist.
  • Upslope fog - forms when moist air is forced up rising terrain and cools.
  • Steam fog - forms when cold air moves over warmer water, the water 'steaming' into the cold air.

All fog destroys visibility and will ground a Part 107 flight below the 3 SM minimum.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Wind and gusts are the everyday sUAS hazard; compare reported/forecast wind AND gusts to your aircraft's limit, and remember wind increases with altitude.
Buildings, ridges, and trees create invisible mechanical turbulence on the lee side - anticipate it from wind direction and obstacle layout.
Thunderstorms develop in three stages - cumulus (updrafts), mature (most violent, updrafts + downdrafts), dissipating (downdrafts); avoid them entirely.
A microburst is an intense localized downdraft producing extreme, rapidly reversing wind shear - one of the most dangerous conditions for a small UA, sometimes with no visual warning.
Fog (radiation, advection, upslope, steam) forms when the temperature-dewpoint spread closes and destroys the visibility needed to fly.

flashcards.definition: A sudden, brief increase in wind speed. Gusts can overpower a lightweight drone, push it off course, or exceed the airframe's maximum wind rating; reported in METARs with a 'G' (e.g., 15G25KT).
Swirling, invisible eddies created when wind flows over and around buildings, trees, hills, and ridgelines; strongest on the downwind (lee) side in strong, unstable winds.

Cumulus (building) stage = updrafts dominate; Mature stage = updrafts and downdrafts coexist, most violent (heavy rain/hail, lightning, strongest shear); Dissipating stage = downdrafts dominate and the storm weakens.

An intense, localized downdraft from a storm or shower that hits the surface and spreads outward, creating extreme, rapidly reversing wind shear over a small area for a few minutes - a severe hazard to sUAS, often with little visual warning.
A sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance (horizontal or vertical). It can instantly destabilize a small drone and is most extreme in and near microbursts.
Fog that forms on clear, calm nights when the ground radiates heat and cools the surface air to its dewpoint; common in valleys near dawn and usually burns off after sunrise.

images_needed.caption: Cross-section of a microburst: an intense downdraft column striking the ground and spreading outward as a damaging horizontal outflow, with arrows showing the rapidly reversing wind shear an aircraft would encounter passing through it.

Three side-by-side cloud cross-sections labeled cumulus, mature, and dissipating, with arrows showing updrafts in the cumulus stage, mixed updrafts/downdrafts and precipitation in the mature stage, and downdrafts in the dissipating stage.

lessons.title: Reading a METAR & TAF; Weather Sources
learning_objectives: Decode every element of a METAR: type, station, time, modifier, wind, visibility, weather, sky condition, temperature/dewpoint, altimeter, and remarks.
Read a TAF, including the valid period and the FM, BECMG, TEMPO, and PROB change groups.
Name the correct FAA weather sources and the products available from each.

content.html:
METAR: the current observation

A METAR is a routine, hourly surface weather observation for an airport. (A SPECI is the same format issued off-schedule when conditions change significantly.) All aviation weather uses Zulu / Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), and the report is read left to right in a fixed order. Consider this sample, which we will decode element by element:

METAR KSAT 121753Z AUTO 27015G25KT 10SM FEW050 SCT250 27/12 A2992 RMK AO2

content.alt: A sample METAR and TAF with each coded element labeled
content.caption: Figure: An annotated sample METAR and TAF, with arrows labeling each element (station, time, wind, visibility, weather, sky condition, temperature/dewpoint, altimeter, remarks; and the TAF valid period and FM/TEMPO/BECMG groups).

rows: ICAO identifier - San Antonio Intl (4-letter, K = contiguous US)
12th day of the month, 1753 Zulu (UTC) - always Zulu
Automated station with no human augmentation (COR = corrected)
From 270 degrees TRUE at 15 knots, gusting to 25 knots
No significant present weather (e.g., RA rain, BR mist, FG fog, TS thunderstorm; - light, + heavy)
Few clouds at 5,000 ft AGL; scattered at 25,000 ft AGL (no ceiling here)
Temperature 27 degrees C, dewpoint 12 degrees C (M would prefix a minus value)
Remarks: AO2 = automated station WITH a precipitation sensor (AO1 = without)

content.html:
Reading the key groups carefully

A few groups trip people up:

  • Wind - the first three digits are direction in degrees true (not magnetic), the next two/three are speed in knots, and a 'G' marks the gust. VRB means variable direction; 00000KT means calm.
  • Sky condition height - in hundreds of feet AGL. The ceiling is the lowest BKN or OVC layer; FEW and SCT are not ceilings.
  • Temperature/dewpoint - in degrees Celsius, separated by a slash; an M prefix means minus (e.g., M03 = -3 degrees C). A small spread warns of fog/low cloud.
  • Altimeter - the 'A' group is inches of mercury with an implied decimal (A2992 = 29.92 inHg).

content.text: Zulu to local: METAR/TAF times are always UTC (Zulu). To convert, subtract the time-zone offset (e.g., US Central Daylight Time is UTC-5, so 1753Z = 12:53 PM CDT). Aviation never uses local time in the report itself.

content.html:
TAF: the forecast

A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast for the area within about 5 statute miles of an airport, normally valid 24 to 30 hours and issued four times a day. It uses METAR-style coding plus a valid-period group and change groups. Consider:

TAF KSAT 121130Z 1212/1318 27012KT P6SM SCT040 FM121800 30015G25KT P6SM SCT050 TEMPO 1220/1224 5SM TSRA BKN030CB BECMG 1306/1308 32008KT

Here the valid period 1212/1318 means from the 12th at 1200Z through the 13th at 1800Z. P6SM means visibility greater than 6 statute miles.

rows: A rapid, near-instant change at the stated time
FM121800 = new conditions begin 1800Z on the 12th
A gradual change over the stated time window
BECMG 1306/1308 = change occurs between 0600-0800Z on the 13th
Temporary fluctuations expected to last under 1 hour each, within the window
A 30% or 40% probability of the stated conditions
Used for uncertain hazards like thunderstorms

content.html:
Where to get official weather

Pull weather from FAA-recognized sources, not a generic phone app:

  • 1-800-WX-BRIEF (Leidos Flight Service) - phone or online (1800wxbrief.com) standard, abbreviated, and outlook briefings from a flight service provider; also files and tracks information.
  • aviationweather.gov (Aviation Weather Center) - the National Weather Service site (formerly ADDS) for METARs, TAFs, graphical forecasts, AIRMETs/SIGMETs, and PIREPs.
  • EFB apps and B4UFLY - integrate weather with airspace; useful for planning, but verify with the official sources above.

A standard briefing covers adverse conditions, synopsis, current and forecast weather, winds aloft, NOTAMs, and TFRs - everything the preflight required by 14 CFR 107.49 expects you to assess.

content.alt: Screenshot-style layout of the aviationweather.gov and 1800wxbrief weather sources
content.caption: Figure: The official FAA weather sources - aviationweather.gov (Aviation Weather Center) and 1800wxbrief.com (Leidos Flight Service) - showing where a Remote PIC pulls METARs and TAFs.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A METAR is the routine hourly observation (SPECI = special); read it left to right: type, station, Zulu time, modifier, wind, visibility, weather, sky, temp/dewpoint, altimeter, remarks.
Wind direction is degrees TRUE; 'G' marks gusts; sky heights are AGL in hundreds of feet; temp/dewpoint are Celsius with M for minus; A-group is inHg.
All aviation weather is in Zulu (UTC); convert to local by subtracting the time-zone offset.
A TAF forecasts ~5 SM around an airport for 24-30 hours, using FM (rapid), BECMG (gradual), TEMPO (brief <1 hr), and PROB30/40 (probability) change groups; P6SM = visibility greater than 6 SM.
Use official sources - 1-800-WX-BRIEF / Leidos (1800wxbrief.com) and aviationweather.gov (Aviation Weather Center) - for METARs, TAFs, and briefings.

flashcards.definition: A routine, hourly aviation surface weather OBSERVATION for an airport, in Zulu time. (SPECI is the same format issued off-schedule for significant changes.)
Terminal Aerodrome Forecast - a forecast for ~5 statute miles around an airport, valid 24-30 hours, issued ~4x/day, using FM, BECMG, TEMPO, and PROB change groups.
Wind from 270 degrees TRUE at 15 knots, gusting to 25 knots. VRB = variable direction; 00000KT = calm; the G marks a gust.
Coordinated Universal Time, used for all aviation weather (the 'Z' in 121753Z). Convert to local by subtracting your time-zone offset.
TAF change groups: FM = rapid change from a stated time; BECMG = gradual change over a window; TEMPO = temporary fluctuations lasting under 1 hour; PROB30/40 = 30% or 40% probability of conditions.
1-800-WX-BRIEF / Leidos Flight Service (1800wxbrief.com) for briefings, and aviationweather.gov (Aviation Weather Center, formerly ADDS) for METARs, TAFs, AIRMETs/SIGMETs, and PIREPs.

images_needed.caption: An annotated sample METAR and TAF: the METAR 'KSAT 121753Z AUTO 27015G25KT 10SM FEW050 SCT250 27/12 A2992 RMK AO2' with callout labels on each element, and below it a TAF showing the valid-period group and FM/TEMPO/BECMG change groups labeled.
A composite of the two official FAA weather websites - aviationweather.gov and 1800wxbrief.com - with the METAR and TAF panels highlighted to show where a Remote PIC pulls observations and forecasts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the sea-level temperature and pressure values of the ICAO standard atmosphere?
options: 15 degrees C and 29.92 inHg
59 degrees C and 30.00 inHg
0 degrees C and 29.92 inHg
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 15 degrees C and 29.92 inHg
quiz_questions.explanation: The standard atmosphere is defined as 15 degrees C (59 degrees F) and 29.92 inHg at sea level. '59 degrees C' confuses the Fahrenheit value with Celsius, and 0 degrees C is not the standard temperature.

quiz_questions.question_text: A METAR reports a temperature of 12 degrees C and a dewpoint of 11 degrees C. What does this small temperature-dewpoint spread most likely indicate?
options: Near-saturated air with a high likelihood of fog or low clouds
Very dry air and excellent visibility
An approaching cold front with thunderstorms
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Near-saturated air with a high likelihood of fog or low clouds
quiz_questions.explanation: A small spread means the air is close to saturation, so fog, mist, or low ceilings are likely. A large spread, not a small one, indicates dry air. A small spread by itself does not specifically indicate a cold front.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which layer of the atmosphere do essentially all weather and all small UAS operations occur?
options: The troposphere
The stratosphere
The ozone layer
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The troposphere
quiz_questions.explanation: The troposphere (surface to about 36,000 ft) is where nearly all weather and all sUAS flight take place. The stratosphere lies above it and contains the ozone layer, which is not where drones fly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the dewpoint temperature represent?
options: The temperature to which air must be cooled to become saturated and begin condensing
The current air temperature minus the wind chill
The highest temperature the air reached during the day
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The temperature to which air must be cooled to become saturated and begin condensing
quiz_questions.explanation: Dewpoint is the temperature at which air reaches 100% relative humidity and water vapor begins to condense into cloud, fog, or dew. The other choices describe unrelated concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Density altitude is best defined as:
options: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature
The height of the aircraft above ground level
The altitude shown when the altimeter is set to the local altimeter setting
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature
quiz_questions.explanation: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature (and humidity) - the altitude the aircraft 'feels.' Height above ground is AGL altitude, and the local-altimeter-setting reading is indicated altitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which combination of conditions produces the HIGHEST density altitude and the worst small-UAS performance?
options: High elevation, high temperature, and high humidity
Low elevation, low temperature, and low humidity
Sea level, standard temperature, and dry air
quiz_questions.correct_answer: High elevation, high temperature, and high humidity
quiz_questions.explanation: 'High, hot, and humid' all thin the air and raise density altitude, reducing lift and thrust. Cold, low, dry conditions give a low density altitude and the best performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a hot summer afternoon at a high-elevation field, how will a multirotor drone's performance most likely be affected compared with a cold morning at sea level?
options: Reduced lift and thrust, slower climb, and shorter flight time
Increased lift and longer flight time because warm air is more energetic
No change, because density altitude does not affect electric drones
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reduced lift and thrust, slower climb, and shorter flight time
quiz_questions.explanation: High density altitude thins the air, so the propellers move less mass per revolution: lift, thrust, climb rate, payload, and endurance all decrease. Electric multirotors still depend on air density for lift, so they are affected.

quiz_questions.question_text: Physically, why does high density altitude reduce a drone's lift and thrust?
options: The thinner air means each propeller stroke accelerates less mass of air downward
The motors overheat and automatically reduce power
Higher humidity makes the propellers heavier
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The thinner air means each propeller stroke accelerates less mass of air downward
quiz_questions.explanation: Lift and thrust depend on air density; in thin air each prop revolution moves less air mass, so less force is generated. Motor overheating and 'heavier props' are not the mechanism behind density-altitude performance loss.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which set of characteristics is typical of UNSTABLE air?
options: Cumuliform clouds, showery precipitation, good visibility, and turbulence
Stratiform clouds, steady precipitation, poor visibility, and smooth air
An inversion, fog, and trapped haze
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Cumuliform clouds, showery precipitation, good visibility, and turbulence
quiz_questions.explanation: Unstable air (steep lapse rate) builds cumuliform clouds, produces showers, usually has good visibility, and is turbulent. The other choices describe stable air, where lifted parcels sink back and conditions are smooth with poor visibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: What effect does a temperature inversion typically have on visibility near the surface?
options: It reduces visibility by trapping haze, smoke, and moisture beneath the warm layer
It improves visibility by lifting pollutants away from the surface
It has no effect on visibility
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It reduces visibility by trapping haze, smoke, and moisture beneath the warm layer
quiz_questions.explanation: An inversion (warm air over cool air) is very stable and acts like a lid, trapping haze, smoke, and moisture near the surface and often producing fog. It degrades, not improves, visibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fast-moving cold front is approaching your operating site. What weather should you expect as it passes?
options: A sudden wind shift, gusty winds, and a narrow band of showers or thunderstorms
A long period of light, steady drizzle and gradually lowering ceilings
Clear, calm, stable conditions ideal for flight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A sudden wind shift, gusty winds, and a narrow band of showers or thunderstorms
quiz_questions.explanation: Cold fronts lift warm air steeply, producing a narrow band of often-violent weather with a sharp wind shift and gusts, then clearing behind. Long steady drizzle with lowering ceilings is the warm-front pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: A warm front is moving into the area. Which conditions are most characteristic of it?
options: Widespread layered clouds, low ceilings, steady precipitation, and poor visibility ahead of it
A short, violent band of thunderstorms followed by rapid clearing
Strong gusty downbursts and rapidly reversing winds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Widespread layered clouds, low ceilings, steady precipitation, and poor visibility ahead of it
quiz_questions.explanation: Warm air rides up a shallow slope over retreating cold air, producing broad stratiform clouds, low ceilings, steady precipitation, and reduced visibility well ahead of the front. The violent, narrow pattern describes a cold front; gusty downbursts describe a microburst.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is an occluded front formed?
options: A fast cold front overtakes a slower warm front, lifting the warm air off the surface
Two warm air masses merge over an ocean
A front stalls with neither air mass advancing
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A fast cold front overtakes a slower warm front, lifting the warm air off the surface
quiz_questions.explanation: An occlusion occurs when a faster cold front catches up to a warm front and forces the warm air aloft, combining traits of both. A stalled, non-advancing boundary is a stationary front.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a METAR, the sky condition is reported as 'SCT020 BKN040 OVC080.' What is the ceiling?
options: 4,000 ft AGL
2,000 ft AGL
8,000 ft AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 4,000 ft AGL
quiz_questions.explanation: A ceiling is the lowest broken (BKN) or overcast (OVC) layer. SCT020 (scattered) does not count, so the ceiling is the BKN040 layer at 4,000 ft AGL.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which METAR sky-cover abbreviation indicates a coverage of 5 to 7 oktas (and can therefore constitute a ceiling)?
options: BKN (broken)
SCT (scattered)
FEW (few)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: BKN (broken)
quiz_questions.explanation: BKN (broken) is 5-7 oktas and, as the lowest BKN/OVC layer, forms a ceiling. SCT is 3-4 oktas and FEW is 1-2 oktas; neither is a ceiling.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under 14 CFR 107.51, what is the minimum flight visibility for small UAS operations, measured from the control-station location?
options: 3 statute miles
1 statute mile
5 statute miles
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 3 statute miles
quiz_questions.explanation: Part 107 requires at least 3 statute miles of flight visibility from the control station. One statute mile is too low and five is not the regulatory minimum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the minimum cloud-clearance distances required for a Part 107 operation?
options: 500 ft below the clouds and 2,000 ft horizontally from the clouds
1,000 ft below and 500 ft horizontally from the clouds
500 ft above and 1,000 ft below the clouds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 500 ft below the clouds and 2,000 ft horizontally from the clouds
quiz_questions.explanation: Part 107 requires staying at least 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds. The other options scramble the standard values; the correct figures are 500/2,000.

quiz_questions.question_text: A METAR reports a broken ceiling at 400 ft AGL (BKN004) and 2 SM visibility. Can you legally conduct a routine Part 107 flight up to 400 ft AGL?
options: No - visibility is below 3 SM and the low ceiling prevents the required 500 ft cloud clearance
Yes - 400 ft AGL is exactly the legal altitude, so the flight is permitted
Yes - cloud clearance rules do not apply below 500 ft AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No - visibility is below 3 SM and the low ceiling prevents the required 500 ft cloud clearance
quiz_questions.explanation: Visibility of 2 SM is below the 3 SM minimum, and a 400-ft ceiling makes it impossible to stay 500 ft below the clouds while still flying. Cloud-clearance and visibility minimums apply throughout the operation, not just above 500 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is a microburst considered one of the most serious hazards for a small unmanned aircraft?
options: It is an intense localized downdraft producing extreme, rapidly reversing wind shear that can overpower the drone
It permanently demagnetizes the drone's compass from a distance
It only forms in clear, calm air and is therefore unexpected on sunny days
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is an intense localized downdraft producing extreme, rapidly reversing wind shear that can overpower the drone
quiz_questions.explanation: A microburst slams down and spreads out, creating extreme, rapidly changing winds that can exceed a small drone's control authority in seconds. It is associated with convective activity (storms/showers/virga), not clear calm air, and it does not demagnetize compasses.

quiz_questions.question_text: During which stage of a thunderstorm are the strongest turbulence and wind shear typically present?
options: The mature stage
The cumulus (building) stage
The dissipating stage
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The mature stage
quiz_questions.explanation: The mature stage has coexisting updrafts and downdrafts, heavy precipitation, lightning, and the most violent turbulence and shear. The cumulus stage is dominated by updrafts and the dissipating stage by downdrafts as the storm weakens.

quiz_questions.question_text: You plan to inspect a rooftop on a windy day. Where is mechanical turbulence most likely to be strongest?
options: On the downwind (lee) side of the building
Directly upwind of the building in undisturbed flow
Far away from any structures over open ground
quiz_questions.correct_answer: On the downwind (lee) side of the building
quiz_questions.explanation: Wind flowing over and around a building creates swirling eddies on its downwind (lee) side, the strongest mechanical turbulence. Upwind flow is comparatively smooth, and open ground far from obstacles has the least mechanical turbulence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Radiation fog most commonly forms under which conditions?
options: Clear, calm nights when the ground cools the surface air to its dewpoint
Strong winds carrying warm moist air over a cold sea
Air being forced up the side of a mountain
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Clear, calm nights when the ground cools the surface air to its dewpoint
quiz_questions.explanation: Radiation fog forms overnight under clear, calm skies as the ground radiates heat and cools the air to saturation; it typically burns off after sunrise. Warm moist air over a cold surface is advection fog, and air lifted up terrain produces upslope fog.

quiz_questions.question_text: Before launching a small drone with a manufacturer wind limit of about 22 mph, the report shows winds gusting to 30 mph. What is the appropriate decision?
options: Do not fly - the gusts exceed the aircraft's wind limit and can cause loss of control
Fly anyway, because gusts are brief and do not count toward the limit
Fly, but only above 200 ft AGL where wind is calmer
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Do not fly - the gusts exceed the aircraft's wind limit and can cause loss of control
quiz_questions.explanation: Gusts can overpower a lightweight drone and must be compared to the aircraft's limit; 30 mph gusts exceed a 22 mph limit. Wind generally increases, not decreases, with altitude, so flying higher would not help.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the METAR group '27015G25KT,' what is the wind doing?
options: Blowing from 270 degrees true at 15 knots, gusting to 25 knots
Blowing toward 270 degrees at 15 knots with a steady 25-knot average
Variable in direction at 27 knots gusting 1,525 knots
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Blowing from 270 degrees true at 15 knots, gusting to 25 knots
quiz_questions.explanation: The first three digits are the direction the wind is coming FROM in degrees true (270), the next two are the sustained speed (15 kt), and the G group is the gust (25 kt). Wind direction always describes where the wind comes from.

quiz_questions.question_text: A METAR includes the group '121753Z.' What does it tell you?
options: The observation was taken on the 12th day at 1753 Zulu (UTC)
The temperature is 12 degrees and the dewpoint is 17 degrees
The visibility is 12 miles at 1753 local time
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The observation was taken on the 12th day at 1753 Zulu (UTC)
quiz_questions.explanation: The date/time group is two digits for the day (12) and four digits for the time in Zulu/UTC (1753Z). It is never local time, and it is not temperature or visibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a METAR, the group 'A2992' refers to what?
options: An altimeter setting of 29.92 inches of mercury
An air temperature of 29.92 degrees C
An altitude restriction of 2,992 ft
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An altimeter setting of 29.92 inches of mercury
quiz_questions.explanation: The 'A' group gives the altimeter setting in inches of mercury with an implied decimal, so A2992 = 29.92 inHg. It is not a temperature or an altitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: Decode the temperature/dewpoint group 'M03/M06' in a METAR.
options: Temperature -3 degrees C, dewpoint -6 degrees C
Temperature 3 degrees C, dewpoint 6 degrees C
Mist at 3 to 6 statute miles
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Temperature -3 degrees C, dewpoint -6 degrees C
quiz_questions.explanation: The M prefix means minus, so M03/M06 is -3 degrees C temperature over -6 degrees C dewpoint. Without the M it would read positive values; 'M' here is not the weather code for mist (which is BR).

quiz_questions.question_text: In a TAF, what does the 'TEMPO' change group indicate?
options: Temporary fluctuations in conditions expected to last less than an hour within the stated window
A permanent, rapid change to entirely new conditions from a stated time
A gradual change taking place over the stated time window
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Temporary fluctuations in conditions expected to last less than an hour within the stated window
quiz_questions.explanation: TEMPO marks brief, temporary fluctuations (each under an hour) within a window. 'FM' is the rapid permanent change from a time, and 'BECMG' is a gradual change over a window.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an appropriate, FAA-recognized source for obtaining a weather briefing and METARs/TAFs before a Part 107 flight?
options: 1-800-WX-BRIEF / 1800wxbrief.com (Leidos Flight Service) and aviationweather.gov
A generic consumer weather app's daily forecast only
The drone manufacturer's marketing website
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 1-800-WX-BRIEF / 1800wxbrief.com (Leidos Flight Service) and aviationweather.gov
quiz_questions.explanation: Leidos Flight Service (1-800-WX-BRIEF) and the Aviation Weather Center (aviationweather.gov) are the official aviation weather sources for briefings, METARs, and TAFs. Consumer apps and manufacturer sites are not authoritative aviation weather sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a TAF valid-period group such as '1212/1318,' what does it represent?
options: Valid from the 12th at 1200Z through the 13th at 1800Z
A wind of 12 knots from 1,318 degrees
A visibility decreasing from 12 to 13 miles
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Valid from the 12th at 1200Z through the 13th at 1800Z
quiz_questions.explanation: The TAF valid-period group is day/hour-Zulu to day/hour-Zulu: 1212 = 12th at 1200Z, 1318 = 13th at 1800Z. It is not a wind or visibility group.
